metadata.summary: Infrastructure as Code with HashiCorp Terraform: HCL syntax, state management, modules, and security. Pin provider versions; never commit .tfvars with secrets.

content.title: Terraform
content.summary: Terraform codifies infrastructure changes in version-controlled HCL, enabling reproducible environments, drift detection, and auditable change history. Remote state with locking (S3 + DynamoDB) prevents concurrent-apply corruption. Pin required_version and all provider versions in every project; never commit .tfvars files containing secrets.

why.p: Terraform codifies infrastructure changes in version-controlled HCL, enabling reproducible environments, drift detection, and auditable change history. Remote state with locking (S3 + DynamoDB) prevents concurrent-apply corruption.

list.item: Provisioning or modifying cloud resources on AWS, GCP, or Azure
Managing multi-environment infrastructure (dev/staging/prod) from one codebase
Building reusable infrastructure modules shared across projects
Setting up CI/CD pipelines that automate terraform plan and apply
Migrating existing resources into Terraform management via import
Kubernetes workloads — use Helm or Kustomize; Terraform's K8s provider is awkward for app config
Simple one-off scripts — shell or cloud CLI is faster for ephemeral resources
Secrets management — use Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or similar; never store secrets in state
Config management inside VMs — that's Ansible/Chef/Puppet territory

rule.statement: Split configuration into main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, versions.tf, providers.tf, locals.tf, and data.tf.
rule.rationale: Each file has a single responsibility; reviewers and tools know where to look for each element type.

rule.statement: Pin required_version with a minimum bound (e.g., >= 1.6.0) and pin provider versions with ~> constraints. Commit .terraform.lock.hcl.
rule.rationale: Unpinned versions cause silent upgrades that break apply; lock file ensures deterministic runs across machines and CI.

rule.statement: Every variable must have a description. Mark sensitive values with sensitive = true. Add validation blocks for constrained inputs.
rule.rationale: Sensitive flag redacts values from plan output and state display. Validation provides clear error messages earlier than resource creation.

example.title: Variable with validation and sensitive flag

rule.statement: Use locals to compute derived values and naming prefixes. Never repeat the same expression in multiple resources.
rule.rationale: DRY locals prevent inconsistent naming and make bulk renames a one-line change.

example.title: Locals with name prefix and config map

rule.statement: Mark outputs that contain secrets as sensitive = true. Provide descriptions for all outputs.
rule.rationale: Sensitive outputs are redacted in CI logs and terraform output commands.

rule.statement: Prefer for_each over count for resources that may be updated mid-lifecycle to avoid index-based drift.
rule.rationale: count uses numeric indexes; removing item[1] of 3 forces destroy/recreate of item[2]. for_each uses stable keys.

example.title: for_each with stable keys and dynamic blocks

section.name: built-in-functions
section.p: Key function categories: string (format, join, split, replace, trimspace), collection (merge, concat, flatten, lookup, keys, values), encoding (jsonencode, base64encode), filesystem (file, templatefile), hash (md5, sha256).

rule.statement: Use merge(local.common_tags, { Name = "..." }) to combine tag maps consistently across all resources.
rule.rationale: Avoids duplicating the common_tags map in every resource block.

section.name: remote-backend

rule.statement: Use remote backend with encryption enabled. For AWS: S3 bucket with SSE-KMS, versioning, public access blocked, and DynamoDB table for locking.
rule.rationale: Local state cannot be shared across team members or CI/CD. S3 versioning enables state recovery. DynamoDB locking prevents concurrent apply corruption.

section.reference: S3 backend with DynamoDB locking template

example.title: S3 backend with locking

rule.statement: Isolate state per environment (envs/dev/, envs/prod/) and per component (network/, compute/). Never share a single state file across environments.
rule.rationale: Shared state means a broken dev apply can corrupt prod state. Isolation contains blast radius.

list.item: terraform state list — list all resources
terraform state show <resource> — show resource details
terraform state mv <old> <new> — rename without destroy
terraform state rm <resource> — remove from state without destroying
terraform import <resource> <id> — import existing resource

rule.statement: Use moved blocks in HCL for resource renames, not terraform state mv. Verify with plan showing no changes after the move.
rule.rationale: moved blocks are version-controlled and self-documenting. terraform state mv is a one-off command that leaves no trace in code.

rule.statement: Each module has single responsibility. Required files: main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, versions.tf. Never configure providers inside modules.
rule.rationale: Provider configuration in modules prevents caller from controlling the provider version/region, making modules inflexible.

rule.statement: All module inputs must have descriptions. Add validation rules for constrained inputs. Provide sensible defaults for optional variables.
rule.rationale: Without descriptions, callers cannot understand what a variable does. Validation surfaces errors at plan time, not at the resource API level.

example.title: Module call with version constraint
example.code:
# Good module call with version constraint module "vpc" { source = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws" version = "~> 5.0"
name = "${local.name_prefix}-vpc" cidr = var.vpc_cidr
enable_nat_gateway = true single_nat_gateway = var.environment != "prod"
tags = local.common_tags }

antipattern.description: Using workspaces to separate production from development.
antipattern.reason: Workspaces share backend config and are not suitable for prod isolation. Use separate directories with separate state files instead.

rule.statement: Never store secrets in Terraform code, tfvars files, or state. Reference secrets via data sources (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault).
rule.rationale: State files are stored in S3 and may be logged in CI. Secrets in code are committed to version control. External secret managers provide rotation and audit trails.

example.title: Fetch secrets from AWS Secrets Manager
Secret hardcoded in variable default
example.note: Use AWS Secrets Manager or Vault data source; never provide defaults for secret variables.

rule.statement: Use IAM roles, not users. Apply least-privilege: no wildcard actions except read-only describe calls. Add resource-level conditions where available.
rule.rationale: Roles use temporary credentials. Wildcard actions grant more permissions than required; resource conditions restrict to tagged/named resources.

list.item: CI/CD uses OIDC workload identity — no long-lived access keys in environment variables
No * in IAM Action for mutating operations (create, delete, modify)
Add aws:RequestedRegion condition to restrict to allowed regions
Enable MFA delete on state S3 bucket for production
Run terraform plan and review all changes — no unexpected destroys
Correct workspace/environment selected and backend state is current
Sensitive values not exposed in plan output (check sensitive = true)
Run tfsec or checkov and address critical/high findings
Database changes reviewed: no accidental instance recreation

rule.statement: In CI: run terraform fmt -check, terraform validate, and security scanning (tfsec/checkov) before plan. Save plan as artifact; apply only from protected branch using the saved plan.
rule.rationale: fmt -check catches formatting drift early. Saved plan ensures apply uses exactly the reviewed plan, preventing race conditions if code changes between plan and apply.

list.item: terraform fmt -check -recursive on every PR
terraform validate after init -backend=false
tfsec or checkov as a required CI step
Plan saved as artifact; apply downloads and uses the artifact
Apply only runs on merge to main/protected branch
OIDC authentication — no long-lived credentials stored in CI secrets

template.purpose: Minimal versions.tf pinning Terraform and AWS provider
Project variables template with validation examples
locals.tf with name_prefix and env_config pattern
LLM prompt to generate a Terraform module
LLM prompt for Terraform security review
